narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70 and an LSAT score of 33, you've shown real strengths in your academic background, especially coming from a middle-income family and planning to attend part-time. However, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 57.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven assessment comparing you to thousands of similar students. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the typical student's rank of around 5.5, signaling to the model a potential dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Countering that somewhat was your undergraduate GPA, well above the average of 3.21, which strongly pulled the prediction in the direction of passing by highlighting your college-level consistency. Your LSAT score also nudged things positively toward success, though less powerfully, as it's a bit below the dataset average of 36.7 but still competitive for many who pass. Overall, your lower Year 3 decile outweighed these positives, placing you below the profile of students who typically pass on the first try. Being in the rare part-time track—only about 7% of students—may have played a subtle role too, as the model associates it with added challenges. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, while reflecting on what led to that Year 3 decile drop and addressing it through targeted prep or coursework. Strengthening these areas could shift future predictions in your favor, and many students in similar spots succeed by focusing on consistent performance gains. You're already ahead with that strong undergrad record—building on it strategically will make a difference. Reach out to advisors for bar prep resources tailored to part-time students like you, and don't hesitate to reapply after showing improvement.
